title: What does the "gross" in GDP mean?
teachingGoal: After reading, a person understands that 'gross' means before subtracting the wear and tear on the things we built. They grasp that machines, roads and buildings wear out, that this wear-out has a name (depreciation, or consumption of fixed capital), and that subtracting it turns GDP into NDP, the 'net' figure. They see why economists usually quote the gross number even though the net one is arguably more honest.
mustTeach: depreciation here means the wearing-out of fixed capital: machines, buildings, roads, vehicles
GDP minus depreciation = Net Domestic Product (NDP)

for the latest year, the depreciation figure to use is the gap between GDP and NDP of the SAME year (the 'same vintage' figure provided), which is about 39 to 40 lakh crore; do not use a depreciation number from a different year, it will not tie
depreciation is a real cost, so the net figure is in one sense more honest, yet the gross figure is the headline because depreciation is hard to measure
an aside the reader will feel: GDP is 'gross' in another way too, it does not net out the using-up of nature, only of built capital
featureNumbers: Depreciation, latest year (GDP minus NDP, same vintage)
